workflow.approvalMode: Manual review first, auto-post only after quality thresholds are proven
workflow.postingPolicy: LinkedIn first. Keep published content public and keep internal draft work private until approved.
workflow.draftPipeline: OpenAI draft generation -> approval artifact -> archive sync

archiveNotes: Internal archive state was reconstructed from the current published public archive on 2026-05-16 after the root social-posts.json source file was missing.
Published records are preserved here. If older unpublished drafts are needed again, restore them from separate planning documents or regenerate them.
The public archive opens on authored posts by default, while shares, likes, and reposts remain filterable.

posts.summary: Repost from Hervé Million. Bringing teams together across EMEA always gives a clearer view of where we stand, and where we’re heading. During the last two weeks, we had Quarter Business Review sessions with all our regions. The discussions show...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Bringing teams together across EMEA always gives a clearer view of where we stand, and where we’re heading. During the last two weeks, we had Quarter Business Review sessions with all our regions. The discussions showed that while markets differ, many of the challenges and priorities are shared. The focus remains on making healthcare systems work better for those delivering care.   We aligned on the next quarter and challenged our plans against what teams are seeing on the ground. A clear direction, and a lot to work on.#MedlineEurope

posts.title: The next important AI skill is not just prompting.
posts.summary: The next important AI skill is not just prompting. It is process design. As AI tools move from answering questions to executing workflows, the value shifts. A good AI workflow needs: - clear inputs - clear business ru...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
The next important AI skill is not just prompting.

It is process design.

As AI tools move from answering questions to executing workflows, the value shifts.

A good AI workflow needs:

- clear inputs
- clear business rules
- clear approval points
- clear error handling
- clear success metrics
- clear ownership

This matters more than the model demo.

In forecasting, for example, the workflow might be:

1. Pull current CRM opportunities
2. Check stage quality
3. Compare against historical conversion
4. Identify missing losses
5. Explain variance
6. Draft the management narrative
7. Flag where human review is required

That is not just a chatbot.

That is a decision process with AI inside it.

The companies that win with AI will not be the ones with the most tools.

They will be the ones that redesign work around clear decisions.

posts.title: A large customer is not automatically a good sales target.
posts.summary: A large customer is not automatically a good sales target. That sounds obvious. But many target lists still start with size: Largest customers. Largest hospitals. Largest regions. Largest historical spend. The better...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
A large customer is not automatically a good sales target.

That sounds obvious.

But many target lists still start with size:

Largest customers.
Largest hospitals.
Largest regions.
Largest historical spend.

The better question is:

What does this customer actually buy?

And then:

What do similar customers buy that this customer does not?

That is where buying-pattern analytics becomes powerful.

A useful model can identify:

- customers with similar purchasing behavior
- products commonly bought together
- category gaps
- underpenetrated accounts
- likely next-best products
- margin-relevant opportunities
- customers worth a sales conversation

The output should not be:

“This customer has potential.”

The output should be:

“This customer buys A and B. Similar customers also buy C and D. The missing category has value, the pattern is credible, and the next commercial action is clear.”

That is the difference between a list and a decision system.

Commercial analytics should not just describe the business.

It should help the business decide where to act next.

posts.title: If an analyst came to me with a market sizing model based mainly on hospital bed count...
posts.summary: If an analyst came to me with a market sizing model based mainly on hospital bed count, I would not tell them to throw it away. But I would tell them not to stop there. Bed count can be a useful starting point, but it...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
If an analyst came to me with a market sizing model based mainly on hospital bed count, I would not tell them to throw it away.

But I would tell them not to stop there.

Bed count can be a useful starting point, but it should not become the main proxy for customer opportunity.

My first question would be:

What does our own customer data already tell us?

Before asking the sales force to collect more external data, I would start with the buying patterns we already have.

Which products are bought together?
Which categories usually appear in mature accounts?
Which accounts are missing products that similar customers already buy?
Which customers behave like peers, even if their bed counts are different?
Which segments show repeatable patterns of adoption, expansion, or under-penetration?

From there, I would build a customer buying-pattern matrix.

Not just customer size.

Customer behaviour.

That matrix could show:

* current product penetration
* category combinations
* product gaps
* account maturity
* peer-group similarity
* cross-sell potential
* historical buying progression
* unusual underperformance versus comparable customers

Then I would use machine learning to classify customers into peer groups based on actual purchasing behaviour.

Because the best comparison is not always:

“Which hospitals have the same number of beds?”

It is often:

“Which customers behave similarly, and what do the stronger-performing peers buy that this customer does not?”

That gives sales a much more practical view of opportunity.

Not a generic market estimate.

A targeted account strategy.

Bed count may tell you the theoretical size of a customer.

Buying patterns tell you how that customer actually behaves.

And good commercial analytics should always move the business closer to action.

posts.title: AI agents are being marketed as digital workers.
posts.summary: AI agents are being marketed as digital workers. That sounds powerful. But inside a real business, the hard question is not: “Can the agent do the task?” The harder questions are: - Does it know the business rule? - D...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
AI agents are being marketed as digital workers.

That sounds powerful.

But inside a real business, the hard question is not:

“Can the agent do the task?”

The harder questions are:

- Does it know the business rule?
- Does it have the right data?
- Who approves the output?
- What happens when the source data is wrong?
- Can we audit what it did?
- Can we measure whether the decision improved?

This is especially important in commercial analytics.

An agent that summarizes a forecast is useful.

An agent that explains why the forecast changed, checks CRM quality, compares sales-cycle assumptions, flags missing opportunities and prepares an executive-ready narrative is much more valuable.

But only if the workflow is designed properly.

The future is not just “AI agents everywhere.”

The future is governed, measurable agents embedded into real decision processes.

posts.title: “Just throw the documents into Copilot.”
posts.summary: “Just throw the documents into Copilot.” I hear this a lot. And it makes me nervous. Not because Copilot or similar AI tools are not useful. They are very useful. But uploading documents and asking for a summary is no...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
“Just throw the documents into Copilot.”

I hear this a lot.

And it makes me nervous.

Not because Copilot or similar AI tools are not useful.

They are very useful.

But uploading documents and asking for a summary is not the same as doing analysis.

Especially when the output includes:

- numbers
- percentages
- financial claims
- customer trends
- forecast movements
- pricing changes
- margin explanations
- executive recommendations

A fluent summary can sound right even when the underlying logic is wrong.

That matters.

Because with numbers, the risk is not just linguistic.

It is analytical.

If AI says “sales declined by 3.7% because of churn,” that is not just a sentence.

It is a claim.

And claims need evidence.

The best thing you can do when using AI on business documents is force it to prove its work:

- quote the source
- show the calculation
- list the documents used
- separate fact from inference
- flag uncertainty
- identify missing data
- show where numbers came from
- explain what it did not check

AI should not just produce an answer.

It should produce an auditable path to the answer.

That is the difference between using AI as a writing assistant and using AI as part of a decision system.

posts.title: Most companies do not have an AI problem.
posts.summary: Most companies do not have an AI problem. They have a decision-system problem. The dashboard exists. The CRM exists. The forecast exists. The pricing file exists. The margin report exists. But the logic underneath is...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
Most companies do not have an AI problem.

They have a decision-system problem.

The dashboard exists.
The CRM exists.
The forecast exists.
The pricing file exists.
The margin report exists.

But the logic underneath is often fragmented.

Sales has one view.
Finance has another.
BI is trying to reconcile both.
Leadership wants a simple answer.
The customer reality is more complex than the reporting structure.

This is where commercial analytics should add value.

Not by creating more dashboards.

But by connecting the logic between:

- customer behavior
- product usage
- pricing
- margin
- forecast quality
- CRM discipline
- sales capacity
- executive decisions

AI can help accelerate this.

But only after the commercial logic is clear.

The real work is not just building a model.

It is making sure the model improves the decision.

posts.title: “Can AI suggest the best price for a sales rep?”
posts.summary: “Can AI suggest the best price for a sales rep?” It sounds like a simple question. It’s not. In fact, it’s one of the most complex problems you can give an AI team — not because of the model, but because of the decisi...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
“Can AI suggest the best price for a sales rep?”

It sounds like a simple question.
It’s not.

In fact, it’s one of the most complex problems you can give an AI team — not because of the model, but because of the decisions hiding underneath it.

Before a single line of code, leaders need to be explicit about things like:
• Are we optimizing for margin, revenue, volume, or retention?
• Is success defined at deal level, customer level, or portfolio level?
• How much price risk is acceptable — and where?
• Do we want consistency across reps, or controlled flexibility?
• What happens when a rep disagrees with the recommendation?

If those questions aren’t answered, the AI won’t be wrong.

It will be misaligned.

And misaligned pricing models don’t fail dramatically.
They quietly shift behavior — eroding margins, distorting incentives, and creating false confidence.

This is where many AI initiatives struggle.

Leaders ask:
“Can AI tell reps what price to sell at?”

Stronger leaders ask:
• “What decision do we actually want to improve?”
• “Where should AI guide, and where should humans decide?”
• “What does a good decision look like under uncertainty?”
• “How will we detect when the model is no longer helping?”

The best pricing systems don’t output a single number.

They offer:
• Price ranges, not absolutes
• Risk signals, not instructions
• Context, not commands
• Continuous feedback loops, not static rules

AI doesn’t set the price.

It shapes the decision environment around the person who does.

And when leaders frame the problem this way, pricing stops being an AI experiment — and becomes a durable competitive advantage.

posts.title: Most people treat AI like a smarter Google.
posts.summary: Most people treat AI like a smarter Google. That’s not where the leverage is. The real value appears when you use it as a thinking partner: • You bring context • You bring constraints • You challenge the output • You...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
Most people treat AI like a smarter Google.

That’s not where the leverage is.

The real value appears when you use it as a thinking partner:
• You bring context
• You bring constraints
• You challenge the output
• You decide what survives

Without that, you don’t get intelligence — you get fluent noise.

AI doesn’t make people smarter.
It amplifies how they already think.

Which is why the gap between “AI access” and “AI advantage” keeps growing.

posts.title: A pattern I keep seeing with AI software purchases:
posts.summary: A pattern I keep seeing with AI software purchases: Companies buy capability before they assess fit. An AI model that looks impressive in a demo can quietly fail in production if: • It wasn’t trained on similar data •...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy:
A pattern I keep seeing with AI software purchases:

Companies buy capability
before they assess fit.

An AI model that looks impressive in a demo can quietly fail in production if:
• It wasn’t trained on similar data
• The data distribution shifts
• The edge cases matter (they usually do)
• No one internally can stress-test the assumptions

That’s not an AI problem.
That’s a governance and ownership problem.

What often gets missed is that the real opportunity isn’t in large, generic models at all.

It’s in small, bespoke models that sit directly inside your own data flows:

• Price suggestions and margin-risk flags
• Forecasting ranges instead of point estimates
• Early warning signals when markets move faster than margins
• Customer grouping based on buying behavior (no manual segmentation)
• Customer-at-risk detection before churn appears in KPIs
• Backorder and supply-stress flagging before it hits revenue

These models don’t need hype.
They need context, feedback, and ownership.

AI works best when:
• The data is yours
• The decision boundary is clear
• The outcome is measurable
• Humans remain accountable

Buying AI without the right people to assess it is like buying a race car without a driver.

Powerful.
Expensive.
And very easy to crash.

The teams getting real value from AI aren’t asking “What tool should we buy?”

posts.title: One thing I’ve learned using AI heavily: There are many things it’s bad at, and pretend...
posts.summary: One thing I’ve learned using AI heavily: There are many things it’s bad at, and pretending otherwise slows teams down. I don’t use AI to: • Decide strategy • Resolve ambiguity • Replace domain judgment • Make final ca...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: One thing I’ve learned using AI heavily: There are many things it’s bad at, and pretending otherwise slows teams down. I don’t use AI to: • Decide strategy • Resolve ambiguity • Replace domain judgment • Make final calls that carry accountability I do use it to: • Stress-test assumptions • Surface second-order effects • Explore edge cases quickly • Compress thinking cycles AI accelerates thinking. It doesn’t own outcomes. The fastest teams aren’t the ones using AI everywhere. They’re the ones who know exactly where not to use it.

posts.title: Jason shared: This isn’t “time spent chatting.” It’s deliberate use. Top 0.1% of users. ~10,000 messa...
posts.summary: This isn’t “time spent chatting.” It’s deliberate use. Top 0.1% of users. ~10,000 messages. Hundreds of focused conversations. Very few images. Almost no fluff. What this really represents is how I use AI: • To think...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: This isn’t “time spent chatting.” It’s deliberate use. Top 0.1% of users. ~10,000 messages. Hundreds of focused conversations. Very few images. Almost no fluff. What this really represents is how I use AI: • To think, not to replace thinking • To pressure-test ideas, not generate noise • To design systems, not chase prompts • To clarify strategy, architecture, and trade-offs • To move faster after I understand the problem Most AI usage today optimizes for novelty. Real value comes from alignment: objectives, constraints, and feedback loops. AI doesn’t fail — objectives do. Reasoning is cheap. Integration is hard. Used properly, AI becomes a force multiplier for judgment, not a substitute for it. The gap between “AI adoption” and “AI advantage” is still very wide. And that gap is where the work is.

posts.title: Most companies are adopting AI. Very few are integrating it. Adoption looks like: • add...
posts.summary: Most companies are adopting AI. Very few are integrating it. Adoption looks like: • adding an LLM to a workflow • building a chatbot • generating content faster • “AI” features bolted onto existing processes Integrati...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Most companies are adopting AI. Very few are integrating it. Adoption looks like: • adding an LLM to a workflow • building a chatbot • generating content faster • “AI” features bolted onto existing processes Integration looks like: • clear decision rights (who owns outcomes) • real feedback loops (what happens when it’s wrong) • strong evaluation (not vibes-based demos) • the right model for the task (often not an LLM) • monitoring, drift detection, retraining or policy updates This is where people overestimate their understanding. They think AI projects are software launches. They’re not. Software is deterministic. AI is probabilistic — and will shift as the world shifts. If you treat AI like normal software, you’ll get a normal outcome: a clever pilot that never becomes reliable enough to matter.

posts.title: We’re entering a weird era where “reasoning” is becoming cheap. You can buy impressive...
posts.summary: We’re entering a weird era where “reasoning” is becoming cheap. You can buy impressive reasoning output on demand. But alignment — getting systems to consistently serve the right intent in the real world — is still ha...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: We’re entering a weird era where “reasoning” is becoming cheap. You can buy impressive reasoning output on demand. But alignment — getting systems to consistently serve the right intent in the real world — is still hard. Because alignment isn’t about cleverness. It’s about: • incentives (loss/reward functions) • context (what “good” means here) • constraints (safety, compliance, brand, risk) • feedback loops (how the system learns from outcomes) That’s why many AI pilots look great in a sandbox, then fall apart in production. The capability is there. The integration isn’t. If you want AI to work, stop asking “Can it reason?” Ask “Can we define success, constrain it, and measure impact after deployment?”

posts.title: Jason liked: Excited to share that I have been appointed Vice President, Sales and Marketing EMEA at...
posts.summary: Excited to share that I have been appointed Vice President, Sales and Marketing EMEA at Medline. Over the past several years, I have had the opportunity to work closely with outstanding teams across Europe, the Middle...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Excited to share that I have been appointed Vice President, Sales and Marketing EMEA at Medline. Over the past several years, I have had the opportunity to work closely with outstanding teams across Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Most recently, as Vice President, Sales and General Manager MEA, I gained valuable experience across diverse markets and healthcare systems. This experience continues to shape my leadership approach. In this new role, I look forward to collaborating even more closely with our EMEA teams to strengthen our sales strategy, support sustainable growth, and remain responsive to the evolving needs of healthcare providers throughout the region. Thank you to the Medline leadership team for their trust and support, and to our teams across EMEA. I am excited about what we will achieve together. https://lnkd.in/eDWfMdK8

posts.title: lot of “AI failures” aren’t model failures. They’re objective failures. Teams optimise...
posts.summary: lot of “AI failures” aren’t model failures. They’re objective failures. Teams optimise what’s measurable: • accuracy • response time • click-through • cost reduction …and then act surprised when the system produces: •...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: lot of “AI failures” aren’t model failures. They’re objective failures. Teams optimise what’s measurable: • accuracy • response time • click-through • cost reduction …and then act surprised when the system produces: • confident nonsense • misaligned decisions • short-term wins and long-term damage That’s not the AI being “wrong.” That’s the AI doing exactly what you asked—just not what you meant. In business, the real question isn’t “Is the model good?” It’s: • What is it rewarded for? • What constraints are explicit? • What trade-offs are encoded? (speed vs quality, growth vs margin, automation vs risk) Most people think they can assess AI projects by demo quality. Demos are theatre. AI success is usually determined before training starts — in the objective function, data definitions, and feedback loop design.

posts.summary: We have officially opened our new office in Riyadh, marking our first permanent presence in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and a major milestone for Medline in the region. Our new Riyadh office strengthens our ability to...

platforms.copy: We have officially opened our new office in Riyadh, marking our first permanent presence in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and a major milestone for Medline in the region. Our new Riyadh office strengthens our ability to support healthcare providers with faster response times, closer collaboration and on-the-ground expertise. The Riyadh team brings together colleagues across Management, Business Development, Product Management, Regulatory Affairs and Human Resources, with support available in both Arabic and English.

posts.title: Most people think “AI” means LLMs. That’s like thinking “transport” means planes. LLMs...
posts.summary: Most people think “AI” means LLMs. That’s like thinking “transport” means planes. LLMs are great at language-shaped work: • drafting, summarising, rewriting • search + synthesis (with sources) • turning messy human in...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Most people think “AI” means LLMs. That’s like thinking “transport” means planes. LLMs are great at language-shaped work: • drafting, summarising, rewriting • search + synthesis (with sources) • turning messy human intent into structured outputs • copilots for analysis, coding, documentation But a huge chunk of business value still comes from non-LLM AI: • forecasting (time series, causal models) • propensity / churn (classification, uplift) • pricing (elasticity, constrained optimisation) • fraud / anomaly detection (statistical + ML) • recommendations (ranking, retrieval) • operations (OR, simulation, queuing, routing) Where AI tends to disappoint is where leaders think it’s intelligent: • ownership of decisions • accountability • “understanding” strategy or nuance • high-stakes judgement without feedback loops If you want real ROI, stop asking “Which LLM?” Start asking: “Which problem class is this?” Because the best solution is often not an LLM — it’s the right model + the right workflow + the right controls.

posts.title: Why mistaking tools for intelligence is risky — especially in business The biggest risk...
posts.summary: Why mistaking tools for intelligence is risky — especially in business The biggest risk with modern AI in business isn’t job replacement. It’s misalignment. LLMs are powerful tools, but they don’t understand your stra...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Why mistaking tools for intelligence is risky — especially in business The biggest risk with modern AI in business isn’t job replacement. It’s misalignment. LLMs are powerful tools, but they don’t understand your strategy, your risk tolerance, or your downstream consequences. They optimize their objective function — not your desired outcomes. When we treat these systems as “intelligent,” we start: • delegating judgment instead of decisions • trusting outputs without validating incentives • assuming correctness where there is only optimization This is where business risk appears. Using AI well isn’t about plugging in the most capable model. It’s about using the right tool, in the right place, with the right loss/reward function. If the objective function doesn’t encode what you actually care about: • profit vs growth • speed vs accuracy • exploration vs control the system will optimize the wrong thing — very efficiently. That doesn’t mean the model failed. It means we misunderstood the tool. Real business intelligence comes from: • clear objectives • explicit constraints • human ownership of outcomes • continuous validation against real-world impact AI should amplify intent, not replace it. Until systems can understand consequences and adapt autonomously, the most important intelligence in the loop is still human.

posts.title: Jason shared: Is it really AI? Large Language Models are often labeled Artificial Intelligence. I thi...
posts.summary: Is it really AI? Large Language Models are often labeled Artificial Intelligence. I think that label is premature. What we mostly have today is the appearance of intelligence. LLMs can produce intelligent-looking outp...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: Is it really AI? Large Language Models are often labeled Artificial Intelligence. I think that label is premature. What we mostly have today is the appearance of intelligence. LLMs can produce intelligent-looking outputs, but they do not: • learn continuously from interaction • revise their internal models through experience • form goals or evaluate outcomes in the world What looks like learning is offline training plus inference over a fixed model. What looks like reasoning is pattern-based inference without understanding. What looks like decision-making is optimization without agency. So where is the line? Appearance of Intelligence - A system shows the appearance of intelligence when it: • generates outputs indistinguishable from intelligent behavior • adapts responses without adapting itself • performs reasoning-like tasks without autonomy or grounding • This is where LLMs sit today. Artificial Intelligence (proper) - A system earns the label Artificial Intelligence when it: • learns from interaction with its environment • updates its internal world model autonomously • evaluates outcomes and corrects itself • pursues goals beyond a single prompt or session • At this point, intelligence is no longer just simulated — it is implemented. Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) - AGI is not “a bigger model” or “better benchmarks”. AGI would be a system that: • learns across domains, not tasks • transfers knowledge without retraining • forms and revises goals • builds persistent memory and identity • understands cause and effect through interaction • improves itself without human orchestration In short: AGI is an autonomous, self-improving learning agent with a grounded model of the world. Until systems cross that threshold, calling them AGI — or even fully intelligent — risks confusing capability with cognition. LLMs are remarkable. But intelligence is not just what a system produces — it’s what it can become on its own. Curious where others would draw this line

posts.title: OpenAI’s Strategy Is Clear — But Is It Sustainable? It’s becoming obvious that OpenAI i...
posts.summary: OpenAI’s Strategy Is Clear — But Is It Sustainable? It’s becoming obvious that OpenAI isn’t just trying to build great models — they’re trying to make their product synonymous with work itself. Integration into every...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: OpenAI’s Strategy Is Clear — But Is It Sustainable? It’s becoming obvious that OpenAI isn’t just trying to build great models — they’re trying to make their product synonymous with work itself. Integration into every workflow, every application, every moment of productivity. A strategy built on ubiquity first, profit later. And they’re willing to lose money for years to make that happen. Because once people, teams, and companies rely on a tool daily, switching becomes almost impossible. But there’s a critical question nobody seems to be asking: Can OpenAI outspend companies like Google, who can embed AI into profitable products at global scale without burning billions? Google doesn’t need to monetise every model directly. They can fold intelligence into Search, Docs, Gmail, Android — instantly deployed to billions. Their cost of distribution is effectively zero. Their moat is enormous. And they can afford to absorb AI costs indefinitely. So while OpenAI races to become the “operating system of work,” Google can simply outlast them. But here’s the bigger point: This entire focus on copilots and productivity tools won’t deliver AGI. Not even close. In my view, the next major leap — the next “Transformer moment” — is far more likely to come from teams like DeepMind, who continue to push the frontier of machine learning beyond language: • protein folding • self-play reinforcement learning • model-based reasoning • novel architectures outside the LLM paradigm These are not incremental productivity improvements. These are scientific breakthroughs that give us entirely new learning principles. OpenAI has chosen the path of productisation. DeepMind has chosen the path of discovery. One creates dependence. The other creates breakthroughs. And if history tells us anything, true paradigm shifts rarely come from the team optimising for market share. They come from the team exploring the edges of what’s possible.#AI#OpenAI#DeepMind#AGI#MachineLearning#Innovation#Strategy#FutureOfWork

posts.title: One of the biggest costs in any business isn’t always on the balance sheet — it’s in th...
posts.summary: One of the biggest costs in any business isn’t always on the balance sheet — it’s in the time and effort that never gets measured. Across large organizations, analysts and teams quietly spend hours every week adjustin...

platforms.name: LinkedIn
platforms.copy: One of the biggest costs in any business isn’t always on the balance sheet — it’s in the time and effort that never gets measured. Across large organizations, analysts and teams quietly spend hours every week adjusting numbers, reformatting data, reconciling mismatched definitions, or reproducing the same reports. From a distance, everything looks fine — dashboards are delivered, metrics are shared, insights circulate. But underneath, enormous manual effort is holding it all together. The real opportunity isn’t just in building more reports — it’s in building smarter processes. When we connect systems, standardize logic, and align how we work, we unlock capacity we didn’t know we had. Data efficiency doesn’t make headlines — but it quietly powers everything that does.#DataEfficiency#DigitalTransformation#AnalyticsLeadership#ProcessImprovement
